item.title: 9 个提高生产力的 shell 工具 | 9 shell tools for productivity
item.description:
所以最近，我花了几天时间尝试在一台新笔记本电脑上重建我的常用环境。 我使用的许多工具变得如此自然，以至于我忘记了它们最初并不存在。 经过几天激烈的记忆练习，我终于设法把所有东西都放在一起。 起初，这篇文章是一个备忘录，让我未来的自己免于更多的大脑俯卧撑。 但后来我认为它可能对其他人有帮助。 看哪，社区，这是我每天使用的工具列表：

我使用zsh shell 因为它是真的很强大。 但是有一种方法可以将它提升到一个新的水平 - oh-my-zsh 。 它带有一堆方便的命令和一个强大的方式来配置和扩展你的 shell插件。 如果您想要不同的贝壳风味，也有主题。

我真的很喜欢它的外观，但我也喜欢它的速度。 它也可以在 shell 启动期间工作，例如，如果它遭受插件膨胀。 说到这，powerlevel10k原生集成了其中的许多。 例如，它可以检测您当前的 Python 虚拟环境或 AWS 用户，并将它们显示在 shell 提示符中。

当命令在终端中完成执行时，这个小工具会触发通知。 不再需要每 5 秒在窗口之间切换来检查 rust 编译！

如果您经常使用环境变量，则必须具备。 您将它们导出到.envrc文件中，并将其放在项目的根目录中。 然后这些变量会在进入该目录时自动加载。 命令行模糊查找器。 我主要将它用于通过命令历史进行反向搜索。 输出结果列表并具有用于搜索的扩展语法。

您可以在一个命令中切换它们中的任何一个。 您还可以将特定版本绑定到目录，每次进入时都会激活该目录。 有一个扩展增加了对虚拟环境的支持。

它不像 CLI 那样冗长，也不像 GUI 工具或 IDE 插件那样臃肿。 我喜欢它，因为它在一个屏幕上提供了我需要的大部分信息，并为常用命令提供了许多快捷方式。 这是一个视频，展示了 Lazygit 可以做的更有趣的事情。

一个简单的脚本，它收集有关文件系统树“移动”的统计信息，并使用它将您传送到最“频繁”的目录。 例如，如果您经常导航到您的项目目录 cd ~/work/company/myapp ，您可以使用z myapp直接跳转。

探索 HTTP 请求的有用工具。 不幸的是，它很少更新，但它做了基本的事情。

这就结束了。 如果您知道其他可以帮助您提高工作效率的惊人工具，请在评论中分享！



原文:

So recently, I spent a few days trying to recreate my usual environment on a new laptop. Many of the tools I use became so natural that I forgot that they weren't there in the first place. After a couple of days of intense memory exercises, I finally managed to get everything together. At first, this post was a memo to save my future self from more brain push-ups. But then I thought that it could be helpful to someone else. Behold, community, this is the list of the tools I use daily:

1. Oh My Zsh

I use zsh shell because it's the default option on macOS really powerful. But there is a way to take it to the next level — oh-my-zsh. It comes with a bunch of handy commands and a powerful way to configure and extend your shell with plugins. There are also themes if you want a different shell flavor.

2. powerlevel10k theme


I really like how it looks, but I also like how fast it is. P10K can magically skip a lag between you hitting an enter key and a prompt appearing on the screen. It also works during shell startup, e.g., if it suffers from plugin bloat. Speaking of which, powerlevel10k natively integrates with many of them. For example, it can detect your current Python virtual environment or AWS user and display them in the shell prompt.

3. bgnotify


This small tool fires notifications when a command finishes executing in a terminal. No more switching between windows every 5 seconds to check on rust compilation!

4. direnv


A must-have if you use environment variables a lot. You export them in a .envrc file and place it in the root directory of your project. Then those variables are automatically loaded upon entering that directory. Direnv also provides handy functions for .envrc file, and it can load envs from .env!

5. fzf


Command-line fuzzy finder. I use it mainly with reverse search by command history. Outputs a list of results and has an extended syntax for searching.

6. pyenv


Pyenv lets you install different versions of python on your system simultaneously, without breaking a thing. You can switch between any of them in one command. You can also bind a specific version to a directory, which will activate every time you enter it. There is an extension that adds support for virtual environments.

7. lazygit

Lazygit is a terminal UI for git. It's less wordy than the CLI and not as bloated as a GUI tool or an IDE plugin. I like it because it has most of the information I need on a single screen and provides many shortcuts for frequently used commands. Here is a video that shows more interesting things Lazygit can do.

8. z script

A simple script that gathers statistics on your filesystem tree "movements" and uses it to teleport you to the most "frecent" directories. For example, if you frequently navigate to your project directory cd ~/work/company/myapp, you can use z myapp to jump right in.

9. wuzz


A useful tool for exploring HTTP requests. Unfortunately, it's rarely updated, but it does the basic stuff.

And that wraps things up. If you know other amazing tools that help you be more productive, share them in the comments!
item.author: kazauwa

item.title: DevOps 的挑战 | Challenges of DevOps
item.description:
谈到 DevOps 计划，有许多挑战需要克服。 组织需要重新考虑其结构，以改进完成工作的方法。 然而，许多公司低估了完成 DevOps 转型过程所需的工作量。 根据即将到来的 Gartner 研究，该研究发现，由于与组织学习和变革相关的挑战，到 2020 年，75% 的 DevOps 计划将无法实现其目标。

选择正确指标的过程是一项艰巨的任务。
例如，在没有显着提高质量的情况下提高部署速度并不是一项成就。 有效的 DevOps 计划需要有助于推动智能自动化决策的指标。 然而，许多组织很难解释 DevOps 指标。 如果您不熟悉 DevOps 指标，请考虑参加DevOps 认证培训。

但是我们从哪里开始呢？ 寻找符合吞吐量速度和效率的指标。

资金有限

由于需要进行大规模的组织和 IT 转型——以前独立的团队组成一个团队，改变角色，并经历其他变化，这个过程需要时间。 根据 Pensa 对 IT 经理的一项研究，一家将 Pensa 软件作为 DevOps 成就的最大障碍的公司是：

  • 不那么大的预算（19.7% 的受访者将其列为预算赤字的原因）
  • 过去的系统 (17.2%)
  • 应用程序的复杂性 (12.8%)
  • 管理多环境的难度（11.3%）
  • 公司文化 (9.4%)

复杂

关于治理，集中化和标准化将如何产生更好的结果，还是只会增加扼杀创新的官僚主义层级？ 此外，还有一个改变你的组织的问题：与你合作的团队能否克服他们的阻力和阻力？ 以一种特定的方式将自己从多年的经营中解脱出来？ 他们能否交流他们从其他组织获得的方法和经验教训，然后实施和管理适当的工具？

一旦解除暂停，jyoti_81 将能够再次评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，jyoti_81 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 jyoti_81 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 jyoti_81 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

原文:

There are numerous challenges to overcome when it comes to the DevOps initiative. The organization needs to rethink its structure in order to improve the method by which things get completed. Many companies underestimate the amount of work needed to complete the course of a DevOps transformation, however. According to an upcoming Gartner research the study found that 75% of DevOps initiatives until 2020 won't be able to achieve their objectives due to challenges related to organizational learning and changes.

"Organizational change and learning is crucial to allowing DevOps to thrive. That's why people-related issues are the most difficult issues -- and not the technology aspect " claims Gartner Senior analyst George Spafford.

The process of selecting the right Metrics is a difficult task.
Enterprises who are transitioning to DevOps practices must utilize metrics to track the progress made, track success and identify areas in need of improvements, Forrester notes. For instance, an increase in the speed of deployment without a significant increase in quality isn't an accomplishment. Effective DevOps program requires indicators that help drive intelligent automated decisions. Yet, many organizations have a difficult time interpreting DevOps metrics. If you new to DevOps metrics then consider taking DevOps Certification Training.

But where do we begin? Look for metrics that are in line with the speed and efficiency of throughput.

Limited Funds

DevOps initiatives face other challenges also. Due to the massive organizational and IT transformations that are required--with previously separate teams forming a team, changing roles, and undergoing other changes, the process will require time. According to an study of IT managers from the Pensa, a company that makes software Pensa the top obstacles for DevOps achievement are:

  • Budgets that aren't as large (cited as a reason for the budget deficit by 19.7% of respondents)
  • Systems from the past (17.2%)
  • Complexity of the application (12.8%)
  • The difficulty of managing multi-environments (11.3%)
  • Culture of the company (9.4%)

Complexity

DevOps efforts are often buried in complexities. IT executives may struggle communicating the benefits of their work to senior executives. Concerning governance how will centralization and standardization result in better outcomes, or will it just add layers of bureaucracy that kill innovation? Also, there's the question of changes to your organization: Can the teams you work with overcome their resistance and resistance? dissociating themselves from the years of operating in according to a particular way? Can they exchange their methods and lessons learned from other organizations, and then implement and manage the appropriate tools?

 
✍️ Writing your own article is easy (we even support markdown).

item.title: Base64 的优点 | Base64's goodness
item.description:
前段时间我有一个用例，base64 拯救了我的屁股，我想我会为自己写一个简短的复习，了解它可以做什么，以及它如何成为任何软件工程师的有用工具。

首先，base64 是一个字符集（还记得吗？ ）。 该集合共有 64 个字符：26 个大写和 26 个小写英文字母（A - Z、az）、10 个数字（0-9）和另外两个字符（通常是+和/ ），总共 64 个字符。 这里的每个字符都映射到一个 0-63 的数字。

编码

更重要的是，base64 是一种字节编码。 你给它一堆字节，它会将它们转换成一个只包含这 64 个字符的字符串（在某些情况下，还有一个=符号）。 请注意，这些字节不必只是文本。 您可以提供一个文件或任何随机的字节序列。 编码的目标是将任何字节流表示为可打印的 ASCII 字符，因为它们“简单”并且大多数计算机都支持它们（在西方世界）。

每种编程语言和操作系统都有一堆工具用于转换为 base 64 或从 base 64 转换，但让我们自己做吧。 我们如何在 base64 中编码像hi这样的字符串？

首先，忘记“字符串”部分。 首先要做的是把它写成一个字节序列。 在 Unicode/ASCII 中， h是代码点 104₁₀， i是 105₁₀。 现在我们编码！

  1. 我们将所有内容放在一起，然后将它们分组为 6 位组（如果没有足够的位，则在末尾添加 0）。
  2. 将每个组作为一个新数字并在 base64 字母表中查找。 例如，如果数字是 3，我们将其替换为 base64 中的数字 3，即 D (A = 0, B = 1...)。 完整的字符集在这里。
 // Put everything together 0110100001101001 // Group them by 6 bits. Last group has only 4, so add two zeros. 011010 000110 100100 // Convert them to base64 numbers 011010 = 26₁₀ = a₆₄ 000110 = 6₁₀ = G₆₄ 100100 = 36₁₀ = k₆₄

所以hi是 base64 编码中的aGk 。 您更有可能看到它写为aGk= ，因为 base64 字符串通常用一个或两个= s 字符填充，以确保它们的长度是 4 的倍数（出于验证接收到的字符串是否完整等原因）。

用例

传输二进制数据

您可以使用它通过专为文本设计的机制传输非文本数据（例如文件）。 这些机制通常会破坏或导致数据损坏，因为您将非文本字节传递给它们。 通过base64编码，您可以安全地发送此类数据，然后对其进行解码以获得原始文件而不会丢失任何文件。

事实上，这是我最初的需要。 我正在使用远程服务器，我需要在服务器上生成 PDF 并检查其内容。 我只能访问终端，所以我无法在服务器上打开像 Adob​​e Reader 这样的花哨的应用程序。 我无法使用 SCP 或其他此类工具。 我必须在服务器上生成，然后将其下载到我的机器并在本地打开它。 使用 base64 是：

 heroku run rails r "puts Base64.encode64(thing.generatePdf.string)" | base64 --decode > file.pdf

我们运行thing.generatePdf方法，该方法返回一个StringIO实例，该实例可以保存到文件中，但在这种情况下，我们在其上调用.string以将内容作为字符串读取。 然后我们将其传递给 base64 编码器并将其打印到标准输出（ puts ）。 然后管道（ | ）将该输出传递给我机器上的base64实用程序，该实用程序对其进行解码并将其存储到file.pdf 。 就这样，我可以在本地打开文件。

你可以做的一个技巧是 JSON 文件上传。 您必须切换到多部分请求正文。 但是，如果你真的想坚持使用 JSON，你可以用 base64 对文件进行编码，然后像普通字符串一样传递它。 (Be warned, it'd likely be a very long string.) （请注意，它可能是一个很长的字符串。）

 { "photo" : "N7c2PJAxPJ9mIGFueSBjYXJuYWwgcGxlYXN1cmUu..." }

不过，您可能不应该这样做，但我们会谈到这一点。

绕过不需要的字符

您可能想要使用 base64 的另一个原因是以安全的方式序列化字符串。 例如，假设您有一个任意字符串，并且您想将其作为字符串序列化为带引号的字符串，就像在代码片段中一样：

 const str = " Some string " ; const codeSnippet = `const x = " ${ str } ";` ;

如果str包含双引号，则会产生无效代码：

 const str = ' There are "quotes" here. ' ; const codeSnippet = `const x = " ${ str } ";` ; // Output: // const x = "There are "quotes" here";

解决此问题的一种方法是在插值之前手动转义引号。 另一种选择是改用编码版本，保证没有引号。 不幸的是，您在这里失去了可读性，所以如果这对您很重要，您可能应该手动转义。

序列化为字符串

我最近的另一个用途是在内存中复制一个对象。 本质上，我想从一个环境中复制一些对象（例如Person类的实例和相关对象）并将其作为完全形成的对象导入到另一个环境中。 这些对象很大，所以我没有时间手动复制每个属性。 我记得serialization ，它是为这些事情设计的。 我可以简单地在一个环境中序列化对象，复制输出，然后在另一个环境中反序列化：

 // in one environment $object = ( ... ); // instance of Person echo serialize ( $object ); // in another environment $input = trim ( fgets ( STDIN )); $object = unserialize ( $input ); // instance of Person
 # Pipe serialized output from (1) as input to (2) php env1.php | php env2.php

这在 PHP 中有效，因为 PHP 的序列化算法只生成文本。 但是 Ruby 会产生非文本字节（正如我在serialization上的帖子中所解释的那样），所以这会中断。 解决方案？ 缺点和注意事项

  • 每 3 个字节的数据被编码为 4 个字节的数据，因此大小增加了大约 33%。
  • 您可以使用 base64 强制通过 JSON 上传文件，但您可能不应该使用 . 它占用更多数据并涉及不必要的额外处理（编码和解码）。 编码不是加密。 任何人都可以读取 Base64 字符串。 任何掌握您的 JWT 的人都可以对其进行解码并读取您存储在其中的信息，因此请注意您存储的内容。
  • 您可以将其放在 URL 中，但/字符可能会被误解，因此通常需要对其进行编码。 如果您需要可以按原样放入 URL 的内容，其他编码（例如 base32）可能更适合。

编码很有趣。😄 Base64 不像 UTF-8 那样是字符编码； 它更像是一种“数据编码”。 这是一篇关于我们称为“编码”的不同事物的有趣短文。



原文:

I had a use case some time ago where base64 saved my ass, and I thought I'd write a short refresher for myself on what it can do, and how it can be a useful tool for any software engineer.

First, base64 is a character set (remember those?). There are 64 characters in this set: the 26 uppercase and 26 lowercase English letters (A - Z, a-z), the 10 digits (0-9) and two other characters (usually + and /), making a total of 64 characters. Every character here maps to a number from 0-63.

Encoding

More importantly, base64 is a byte encoding. You give it a bunch of bytes, and it will convert them to a string that contains only those 64 characters (and in some cases, an = sign). Note that these bytes don't have to be just text. You can supply a file or any random sequence of bytes. The goal of the encoding is to represent any byte stream as printable ASCII characters, because they're "simple" and most computers support them (in the Western world).

Every programming language and OS has a bunch of tools for converting to/from base 64, but let's do it ourselves. How do we encode a string like hi in base64?

First off, forget about the "string" part. Base 64 works on bytes, so it doesn't care whether or not the input is a string. The first thing to do is to write this as a sequence of bytes. In Unicode/ASCII, h is the codepoint 104₁₀ and i is 105₁₀. (I'm using ₁₀ to indicate these are base-10 numbers.) Now we write these as base-2 numbers:

And now we encode!

  1. We put everything together, then group them in 6-bit groups (add 0s at the end if there aren't enough bits).
  2. Take each group as a new number and look it up in the base64 alphabet. For instance, if the number is 3, we replace it with number 3 in base64, which is D (A = 0, B = 1...). Full character set here. 
// Put everything together
0110100001101001

// Group them by 6 bits. Last group has only 4, so add two zeros.
011010 000110 100100

// Convert them to base64 numbers
011010 = 26₁₀ = a₆₄
000110 = 6₁₀ = G₆₄
100100 = 36₁₀ = k₆₄

So hi is aGk in base64 encoding. You'll more likely see it written as aGk=, because base64 strings are often padded with one or two =s characters to ensure their length is a multiple of 4 (for reasons such as verifying that the received string is complete).

Use cases

The important thing about base64 is that it can represent any byte in these "regular" text characters, making it useful for a bunch of things:

Transferring binary data

You could use this to transfer non-text data (such as a file) over mechanisms designed for text. These mechanisms would normally break or result in corrupted data, because you're passing non-text bytes to them. By encoding in base64, you can safely send such data and then decode it to get the original file without any loss.

In fact, this was my initial need. I was working with a remote server, and I needed to generate a PDF on the server and check its content. I only had access to the terminal, so I couldn't open a fancy app like Adobe Reader on the server. I didn't have access to SCP or other such tools. I had to generate on the server, then download it to my machine and open it locally. With base64 this was:

heroku run rails r "puts Base64.encode64(thing.generatePdf.string)" | base64 --decode > file.pdf

(This is a Ruby-based example, but the same thing applies everywhere.)

The heroku run rails r bit is the wrapper code to run this command on my remote Rails app via Heroku. We run the thing.generatePdf method, which returns a StringIO instance which can be saved to a file, but in this case, we call .string on it to read the content as a string. Then we pass that into the base64 encoder and print that to stdout (puts). The pipe (|) then passes that output to the base64 utility on my machine, which decodes it and stores it to file.pdf. And just like that, I could open the file locally.

(I'm ashamed to admit that I first tried just printing the original string directly into a file on my machine, but it didn't work. The file was corrupted. Which makes sense, because there are many bytes in PDFs that aren't text, and terminals and SSH are designed for text.)

One trick you can do with this is JSON file uploads. JSON is a text format, so you can't upload files in it. You'd have to switch to multipart request bodies. But, if you really wanted to stick to JSON, you could encode the file in base64 and pass it like a regular string. (Be warned, it'd likely be a very long string.)

{ "photo": "N7c2PJAxPJ9mIGFueSBjYXJuYWwgcGxlYXN1cmUu..." }

You probably shouldn't, though, but we'll come to that.

Getting around unwanted characters

Another reason you might want to use base64 is to serialize strings in a safe way. For instance, supposing you have an arbitrary string, and you want to serialize it as a string into a quoted string, like in a code snippet:

const str = "Some string"; const codeSnippet = `const x = "${str}";`;

This produces invalid code if str contains double quotes:

const str = 'There are "quotes" here.'; const codeSnippet = `const x = "${str}";`;
// Output:
// const x = "There are "quotes" here";

One way you could get around this is to manually escape the quotes before interpolating. Another option would be to use the encoded version instead, which is guaranteed to not have quotes. (I learnt this trick from Caleb Porzio).

const str = 'There are "quotes" here.'; const codeSnippet = `const x = "${btoa(str)}";`;
// Output:
//'const x = "VGhlcmUgYXJlICJxdW90ZXMiIGhlcmUu";

Unfortunately, you lose readability here, so if that's important for you, you should probably escape manually.

Serializing to string

Aanother use I had recently was replicating an object in memory. Essentially, I wanted to make a copy of some object (eg an instance of a Person class, and related objects) from one environment and import that as a fully formed object into another. These objects were big, so I didn't have the time to manually copy each property over. I remembered serialization, which was designed for things like these. I could simply serialize the objects in one environment, copy the output, and deserialize that in the other:

// in one environment
$object = (...); // instance of Person
echo serialize($object); // in another environment
$input = trim(fgets(STDIN));
$object = unserialize($input); // instance of Person

# Pipe serialized output from (1) as input to (2)
php env1.php | php env2.php

This works in PHP, because PHP's serialization algorithm produces only text. But Ruby's produces non-text bytes (as explained in my post on serialization), so this would break. The solution? Base64.

object = (...)
puts Base64.encode64(Marshal.dump(object)) input = gets.strip
object = Marshal.load(Base64.decode64(input))

Downsides and points to note

  • Base64's output is larger than the input. Every 3 bytes of data get encoded to 4 bytes of data, so that's around a 33% increase in size.
  • You can use base64 to force file uploads via JSON, but you probably shouldn't. It takes up more data and involves unnecessary extra processing (encoding and decoding). Multipart is a scheme designed for binary files over HTTP, so favour that.
  • Base64 is not a security mechanism. Encoding is not encryption. Anyone can read a Base64 string. JWTs are base64-encoded, but that's just for ease of transport. Anyone who gets a hold of your JWT can decode it and read the information you're storing in it, so be careful about what you store there.
  • Base64 isn't entirely URL-safe; you can put it in a URL, but the / character might be misinterpreted, so it often needs to be encoded. If you need something that can be put in a URL as-is, other encodings (such as base32) might be a better fit.

Encodings are fun.😄 Base64 isn't a character encoding in the same way UTF-8 is; it's more of a "data encoding". Here's an interesting short post on some different things we know as "encodings".
item.author: shalvah

item.title: SLF4J 和 Java 日志地狱的简要介绍 | A brief intro to SLF4J and the Java logging hell
item.description:
这将打破我用巴西葡萄牙语写的文章链，我已经养成了用英语做学习笔记的习惯，这样更容易分享。 我很快就会在这里翻译所有内容并创建一个翻译版本！

在 Java日志诞生之初，它还没有今天那么重要，而且当时的大多数语言都归结为向控制台写入行，这已经足够好了！ 当内存稀疏时，为什么需要标准和花哨的结构，对吧？

所以经常看到人们使用相当烦人的System.out和System.err并使用自己的解决方案将日志写入文件和其他地方！

这是开源社区首先介入的地方！ 由于 Java 缺乏任何日志记录标准，并且它的“本地记录器”（这里很慷慨，因为它是err的，而且out ...为 Java 提供可扩展、可配置和标准化的日志记录。

如今，log4j 因 2021 年 12 月对整个行业造成严重破坏的安全事件而臭名昭著，但在当时这是一件好事。 不完美，但很好！

当然，当时的 Java 所有者 Sun 很快就知道需要标准和本地方式来记录内容，因此他们决定实现这一目标！

在 2002 年的 JDK 1.4 中， java.util.logging作为日志记录的本机产品发布。 但是……它并没有受到好评。 首先，很难解耦和隔离，然后由于某种未知的原因，他们还决定重新发明轮子并使用一堆随机名称来表示日志级别，而不是行业内的标准化名称。 多亏了这一点，您才有了珍珠，例如能够log.fine("this is fine") ，但也能够log.finer("this is even finer!")更不用说最好的log.finest("really great logging levels, innit?") ...您可能已经注意到他们决定标准化日志级别的方式非常log.severe("not good") ！

所以即使在 Android 中做 Kotlin 的事情，如果你愿意，你仍然可以记录非常fine和severe的消息！

我需要切换记录器！ 怎么办？

既然有两个“流行”的竞争者，一个新问题开始浮出水面：如果我想将我的项目从惊人的java.util.logging库移动到log4j怎么办？

由于这两个库都与它们自己的实现相耦合，这意味着您需要一次重构整个代码，或者在一段时间内使用两个日志库。 您还可以创建自己的接口和实现来隐藏调用者的日志记录细节！ 这就是社区所做的！

它有它的缺点并且没有被广泛采用，但它的想法仍然存在，以看到更好的实现诞生。 输入SLF4J。

实现解耦是因为在我们的实现过程中只需要依赖 SLF4J 接口即可。 这个魔法是可以实现的，因为日志库本身需要提供插入 SLF4J 抽象的“包装器”或“适配器”，然后交付它们的日志功能。

 import org.slf4j.Logger import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory /** * get a SL4J logger and do something */ fun getLoggerAndLog () { val logger : Logger = LoggerFactory . getLogger ( "my logger" ) // note that this is a SLF4J Logger Interface! // whichever library is 'wrapped' by SLF4J now handles the levels, formatting and everything! logger . debug ( "this is a debug message" ) logger . info ( "this is some information" ) logger . warn ( "this is a warning!" ) logger . error ( "this is an error" ) }

实际上，这里发生的情况是，您至少需要将两个依赖项添加到项目中才能使其正常工作：

  1. 提供自己使用Reflection和其他技术来“绑定”到 SLF4J 提供的LoggerFactory类和例程中！

    什么是“门面”

    简而言之，Facade 试图为它下面的复杂系统提供一种更简单（和通用）的访问方法。 这很好，因为它可以允许这些子系统的客户端与它们自身之间的解耦，同时也减少了完全理解这些子系统的需要！

    想象一下你想在网上买东西。 作为开发人员，您知道您只是在网站（或移动应用程序）中输入信息，但您也知道它下面有一些东西。 在每一个电子商务的下面都会有 API、消息和数据库！ 在某种程度上，你可以说前端是下面发生的一切的门面。 作为在线购物者，您不需要知道发生了哪些 API 调用以及通过 RabbitMQ 发送了哪些消息，您只需关心与“购买”例程的交互是否为您带来了所有魔力。

    示例 - Android 上的 Logback！

    首先要做的事情：让我们首先将 SLF4J 依赖项添加到应用程序的 Gradle 文件中：

     /* snip */ dependencies { /* Adding SLF4J's Interfaces */ implementation 'org.slf4j:slf4j-api:1.7.36' }

    现在我们需要添加 Logback 的库，但是由于在 Android 上运行 Logback 需要一些额外的步骤，我们可以使用已经为我们完成所有额外工作的分叉版本： Logback-android 。 现在，为了让 Logback 工作，我们需要首先添加依赖项并将 logback xml配置添加到应用程序的assets中：

     /* snip */ dependencies { implementation 'org.slf4j:slf4j-api:1.7.36' /* Logback-android */ implementation 'com.github.tony19:logback-android:2.0.0' }
     <configuration> <appender name= "logcat" class= "ch.qos.logback.classic.android.LogcatAppender" > <tagEncoder> <pattern> %logger{12} </pattern> </tagEncoder> <encoder> <pattern> [%-20thread] %msg </pattern> </encoder> </appender> <root level= "DEBUG" > <appender-ref ref= "logcat" /> </root> </configuration>

    现在剩下的就是通过调用 SLF4J 中的LoggerFactory.GetLogger方法来请求一个Logger ，然后您就可以退出了！

     lateinit var log : Logger override fun onCreate ( savedInstanceState : Bundle ?) { super . onCreate ( savedInstanceState ) log = LoggerFactory . getLogger ( "test" ) log . debug ( "Initializing, now with 101% more HILT" ) setContent { AppCanvas { Greeting ( log ) } } }

    其他资源

    这篇文章的封面来自https://undraw.co/



原文:

This will be breaking my chain of Articles written in Brazilian Portuguese, I've fallen into the habit of making my study notes in English to make them easier to share. I'll soon translate everything here and create a translated version!

A brief history of logging in Java

At the dawn of Java logging still wasn’t as important as it is today, and as most languages of at that time everything boiled down to writing lines to the console and that was good enough! Why do you need standards and fancy structures when memory is sparse, right?

So it was common to see folks using the rather annoying System.out and System.err and doing their own solutions to write logs to files and other places!

log4j is born

This is where the Open Source community first steps in! Since Java lacked any logging standard whatsoever and it’s “native logger” (being generous here given it was err and out...) was rather poor when it came to features the community itself rose to the challenge and log4j was created as a mean to provide extensible, configurable and standardized logging for Java.

Nowadays log4j is infamous due to the security incident that wreaked havoc across the industry back in December 2021, but at its time it was a good thing. Not perfect, but good!

Of course Sun, the Java owner back then, quickly got wind of a need for standards and native ways to log stuff so they decided to make that happen!

The ‘native’ library enters the fray

With JDK 1.4, in 2002, java.util.logging was released as a native offering for logging. But… it wasn’t well received. First it was hard to decouple and isolate, then for some unknown reason they also decided it’d be nice to reinvent the wheel and use a bunch of random names for log levels instead of the standardized ones the industry had. Thanks to that you had pearls such as being able to log.fine("this is fine") but also being able to log.finer("this is even finer!") and not to mention the best of all log.finest("really great logging levels, innit?")... as you may have notice the way they decided to standardize log levels was quite log.severe("not good")!

🤦🏻 Worth noting that up to this day the java.util.logging library keeps this standard due to backwards compatibility. So even when doing Kotlin stuff in Android you can still log great fine and severe messages if you wish!

I need to switch loggers! Now what?

Now that there were two ‘popular’ contenders a new problem began to surface: What if I want to move my project from the amazing java.util.logging library into log4j?

Since both libraries were coupled to their own implementation that meant you’d need to refactor your whole code at once or roll with two logging libraries for a while. You could also create your own interfaces and implementations to hide the logging details from caller! And that’s what the community did!

Apache common-logging was born to provide means to abstract the implementation details of any logging library and provide a common, standard, API that any Application may use without caring who’s writing the logs behind the curtains! It had its shortcomings and wasn’t that widely adopted, but it’s idea lived on to see a better implementation be born. Enter SLF4J.

SLF4J to the Rescue

Simple Logging Facade for Java (SLF4J for short) is an out-of-the-box set of Interface and Abstractions that aim to consolidate and standardize logging in the JVM environment, while allowing decoupling.

The decoupling is achieved because you need only to rely on SLF4J interfaces during our implementation. This wizardry is achievable because logging libraries itself need to provide “Wrappers” or “Adapters” that plug into SLF4J abstractions to then deliver their logging functionalities.

import org.slf4j.Logger
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory /** * get a SL4J logger and do something */
fun getLoggerAndLog() { val logger: Logger = LoggerFactory.getLogger("my logger") // note that this is a SLF4J Logger Interface! // whichever library is 'wrapped' by SLF4J now handles the levels, formatting and everything! logger.debug("this is a debug message") logger.info("this is some information") logger.warn("this is a warning!") logger.error("this is an error")
}

In practical terms what happens here is that you need at least two dependencies to be added to your project for this to work:

  1. 'org.slf4j:slf4j-api' for the SLF4J interfaces
  2. log4j-slf4j18-impl, com.github.tony19:logback-android, ch.qos.logback:logback-classic for the SLF4J ‘providers’

The provides themselves use Reflection and other technologies to “tie” into the LoggerFactory class and routines provided by SLF4J!

What is a “Facade”

The “Facade” within SLF4J stands for the Facade Design Pattern. In a nutshell a Facade attempts to provide a simpler (and common) access method to complex systems beneath it. This is good because it can allow for decoupling between clients of those subsystems and themselves while also reducing the need to completely understand those subsystems!

Imagine you want to buy something online. As a developer you know that you’re only inputting information in a website (or a mobile app) but you also know that there’s something beneath it. Beneath every ecommerce out there there’ll be APIs, Messaging and Databases! In a way you could say that the Frontend is a Facade for everything that happens underneath. As an online shopper you don’t need to know about which API calls are happening and which Messages are being sent over RabbitMQ, you just care that interacting with the ‘buy’ routine does all the magic for you.

Sample - Logback on Android!

First things first: Let’s start by adding the SLF4J dependency to our Application’s Gradle file:

/* snip */
dependencies { /* Adding SLF4J's Interfaces */ implementation 'org.slf4j:slf4j-api:1.7.36'
}

Now we’d need to add Logback’s library, but since there are some additional steps required to run Logback on Android we can use a forked version that does all the extra work for us already: Logback-android. Now, to get Logback working we’ll need to first add the dependency and add the logback xml configuration to the application’s assets:

/* snip */
dependencies { implementation 'org.slf4j:slf4j-api:1.7.36' /* Logback-android */ implementation 'com.github.tony19:logback-android:2.0.0'
}

<configuration> <appender name="logcat" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.android.LogcatAppender"> <tagEncoder> <pattern>%logger{12}</pattern> </tagEncoder> <encoder> <pattern>[%-20thread] %msg</pattern> </encoder> </appender> <root level="DEBUG"> <appender-ref ref="logcat" /> </root>
</configuration>

Now all that’s left is to request a Logger by invoking the LoggerFactory.GetLogger method from SLF4J and you can Log away!

lateinit var log: Logger override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) log = LoggerFactory.getLogger("test") log.debug("Initializing, now with 101% more HILT") setContent { AppCanvas { Greeting(log) } }
}

Additional Resources

Complete notes available on my Notion workspace.

This post's cover is from https://undraw.co/
item.author: ardc_overflow

item.title: 在 Scrimba 完成 JavaScript 免费课程 | Finished The JavaScript Free Course at Scrimba
item.description:
也许现在开始学习基础知识有点晚了，因为我是一名软件工程专业的学生，​​从来没有真正想过成为一名 WebApp 开发人员，但迟到总比没有好，对吧？ 好吧，至少我认为那样不了解其他人：3 hhh

我想我真的很喜欢 Per 教授这门课程的方式，我从未见过任何一位性格如此热情的老师！ 我觉得正是因为他的热情，我才继续学习这门课程，我很幸运能遇到这个平台。 虽然我的最终目标是学习 Angular 以及后来的一些后端开发，但似乎没有适合他们的课程。

真正让我觉得我真正在学习的是如何使用 JavaScript 我们可以动态操作静态网页！ 我很惊讶，我就像哇，这太酷了！ 只需要 JavaScript 和一些 CSS 和 HTML 代码，我们就可以做很多事情！ 非常酷！ 我真的希望我能成为一名优秀的 WebAppDev 并且是最好的 WebAppDev 之一：> 并祝所有新学习者一切顺利！

哦！ 当我在课程的代码解决方案中发现错误时，我也很高兴。 我注意到的一件事是 Per 构建的 Chrome 扩展有一个小错误，例如当用户按下保存输入按钮时，程序会保存一个空的 [""] 字符串，或者它可能被称为 Null not sure, input in javascript 中的“MyLeads = []”，也可能在 LocalStorage 中，等等，让我快速检查一下！

好的，是的，我刚刚确认它还用于在本地存储中添加一个空字符串，该字符串将占用 myLeads 数组中的索引位置。

所以我在学习如何解决它的过程中想，我尝试使用一些 if 条件，直到我来到“真实和虚假价值”的部分......我立即得到了如何解决这个问题的解决方案。 我稍后使用此代码段修复了它：

 inputBtn.addEventListener("click", function(){ if(inputEl.value) { myLeads.push(inputEl.value); localStorage.setItem("myLeads", JSON.stringify(myLeads)); inputEl.value = ""; render(myLeads); } console.log(myLeads); });

现在它不再存在当按下“保存输入”按钮时字符串会有一堆空字符串值的错误。 我还想在保存时为每个链接添加一些删除按钮，但似乎以我目前对语言的了解，我现在无法弄清楚，但我希望我会因为我的非常好的建议'已经从我们的 Scrimba discord 成员之一收到，以查看列表项数组，我假设它类似于 C++ 中的链接列表。 希望我会尽快调查。 因为我真的很佩服 Per 的教学，并且很高兴能学到更多，所以决定写一篇文章，因为他建议在最后一次训练赛上写一篇文章。 因此，将来我可能会分享更多我的学习旅程。 虽然我的写作风格很随意，但我希望我不会冒犯任何人，每个人都对我放轻松hhh！

我想我真的很喜欢这门课程，现在我将开始免费的 TypeScript 课程，不知道会怎样，但希望我也能从这门课程中学到很多东西。 如果这里的任何人有一些更好的建议或其他平台可以帮助我学习 Angular 以及与 Angular 配合良好的后端的东西，我真的很感激任何积极的反馈。



原文:

Maybe it's a bit late to start learning the basics as I'm a software Engineering student, and never really thought about becoming a WebApp developer but still, it's better to be late than never right? Well at least I think that way don't know about others :3 hhh

I think I really enjoyed the way Per teaches this course I've never seen any teacher with such enthusiastic character! I feel like just because of his enthusiasm I kept on going with this course and I'm blessed that I've come across this platform. Though my end goal is to learn Angular and later some backend dev as well, It seems there are no courses for them.

Things that really made me feel I'm really learning is how using JavaScript we can dynamically manipulate a static webpage!! I was so surprised and I was like wow that's cool! with just JavaScript and some CSS and HTML codes, we can do so many things! so cool! I really hope I can be a good and one of the best WebAppDev out there :> and all the very best to all of the new learners as well!

OH! also I was so happy when I found a bug in the course's code solutions. One thing I noticed was that the Chrome extensions that Per builds have a little sneaky bug for example when the user presses the save input button, the program would save an empty [""] string, or maybe it's called Null not sure, input in the "MyLeads = []" inside the javascript and maybe also in the LocalStorage, wait let me check real quick!

Okay yes, I just confirmed it also used to add in the local storage an empty string which would take up an index place in the myLeads array.

so I was thinking as I was going through the course how to fix it, I tried using some if condition until I came to the part of "truthy and falsey value"... Instantly I got the solution how to fix this issues. I fixed it later using this code snippet:

inputBtn.addEventListener("click", function(){
  if(inputEl.value)
  {
    myLeads.push(inputEl.value);
    localStorage.setItem("myLeads", JSON.stringify(myLeads));
    inputEl.value = "";
    render(myLeads);
  }
  console.log(myLeads);
});

Now it doesn't have the bug anymore where the string would have a bunch of empty string values when pressed on the Save Input button. I also want to add some kinda remove button for each of the links when saved but it seems with my current knowledge of the language I'm unable to figure it out as of now, but I hope I will because of the very good suggestions I've received from one of our Scrimba discord members to look into an array of list items I'm assuming it's similar to Linked List in the C++. I'll look into it soon hopefully. Since I really admire Per's teaching and really excited to learn more that's why decided to write a post because he suggested writing a post on the last scrim. So probably I'll be sharing more of my learning journey in the future. Though my writing style is very informal I hope I don't offend anyone and everyone takes it easy on me hhh!

I think I really enjoyed the course and now I'll start the free TypeScript course not sure how that'll go but fingers crossed hopefully I'll learn a lot from that course as well. If anyone from here has some better suggestions or other platforms that can help me learn Angular and Something that goes well with Angular for the backend as well I'd really appreciate any kind of positive feedback.
